chapters.title: Ukraine sits between East and West yet remains unmistakably itself
chapters.text:
Recent years have thrust Ukraine into global headlines. Before 2022, many outsiders could not locate it on a map. Putin treated it as Russia's extension; Western observers often filed it alongside Central European states — a kind of proto-Poland. Neither frame captures Ukrainian reality. Sociologists in the Nestor group concluded that neither the Russian administrative model nor the Central European one fits: in attitudes toward family, church, and small enterprise, Ukraine tilts closer to Mediterranean societies such as Italy or Greece.

Ukraine is polyphonic — a crossroads where cultures collide and stereotypes break. Citizens navigate bilingualism that challenges Herder's link between language and nationhood; Muslims and Jews cooperate in mutual aid; nationalism coexists with democratic practice. The history is nonlinear and turbulent, yet that vortex produces remarkable individuals.

The Ukrainian does not live thanks to history, with history, or in history, but near history and most often against it. ~ Andrii Bondar

Volodymyr Yermolenko

Even Ukrainians struggle to map their own identity, sensing otherness before they can articulate it. Foreign stereotypes reduce the country to hopak, corruption, boxing champions, Maidan, and war — while overlooking Malevich, Khvylovy, and Skovoroda. Ukraine is not merely an agricultural hinterland; it runs electronic government services, hosts globally known IT firms, exports fashion brands, and produces inventive advertising. Corruption and oligarchy persist, yet young talent and artists carry the nation's name abroad.

Ukraine fascinates because contradictory elements coexist and fuse into something singular.

The essays ahead explore collapsed empires, shattered lives, and people pursuing peace on their own soil through adversity with stubborn optimism.

chapters.title: Fertile land shaped more than half of Ukraine's territory and its history
chapters.text:
Black earth has driven Ukraine's economy and politics for centuries. Rich soil enabled sedentary agriculture among early tribes; Trypil culture emerged from that foundation. Herodotus described Scythian plowmen north of the Black Sea steppe — the first written mention. Nomadic raiders repeatedly defeated farming communities whose lifestyle left them vulnerable. From the eighth through thirteenth centuries, Norse warriors controlled the Varangian-to-Greeks trade corridor, founding Rus with Kyiv at its center under rulers such as Rurik, Igor, and Olga.

Ibn Khaldun theorized cyclical nomadic conquest: invaders assimilate, soften, then fall to fresher nomads. Kyivan Rus followed that arc after feudal fragmentation following Yaroslav's death, culminating in the Mongol sack of Kyiv in 1240. A striking paradox marked early statehood — immense material wealth paired with thin cultural development.

From the fourteenth through eighteenth centuries, Polish rule spread the Ukrainian language and nascent national consciousness. Landlords bound peasants in serfdom to cheapen bread production — a system comparable to plantation slavery in the Americas.

Fugitives from serfdom often joined the Cossacks, farming and raiding in patterns reminiscent of nomads but under Christianity. Rus evolved into Ukraine; after Bohdan Khmelnytsky's uprising, Muscovy seized portions of its territory. Conquest of the Crimean Khanate opened seaborne grain trade to world markets.

Modern Ukrainian culture remained predominantly rural after elites were depleted under Polish and Russian domination.

chapters.title: The Cossacks functioned as Ukraine's frontier warriors — a Viking parallel
chapters.text:
The name Ukraine first appears in the Kyiv Chronicle continuation of The Tale of Bygone Years, meaning borderland — territory on both sides of a frontier. Cossacks arose on that steppe edge; without them, Ukrainian statehood as we know it would not exist. They built the Zaporizhzhia Host, then Hetmanate Ukraine. Turkic in origin, kozak carried meanings from warrior and guard to robber.

Early Cossacks included Tatar fighters, but Zaporizhzhian hosts differed by settling rather than roaming. Armed with muskets in the seventeenth century — the Kalashnikov of their era — they resembled musketeers more than cavalry hordes. The class mixed rebels, soldiers, raiders, and traders.

Hetmans replaced princely titles; Konstantin Ostrozkyi exemplifies the fading princely tradition before Cossack aristocracy assumed military and administrative power in the eighteenth century.

Ukraine, Russia, and Belarus each claim Kyivan Rus heritage — a fact Moscow frequently weaponizes to justify aggression toward Kyiv.

Taras Shevchenko immortalized the freedom-loving Cossack in literature, treating History of the Rus as Ukraine's authentic chronicle.

Were Cossacks a nation or an elite? Historian Mykhailo Hrushevskyi, aligned with Narodnik populism, downplayed aristocratic Cossacks in favor of mass participation. Vyacheslav Lipynskyi, from Polish nobility, countered that elites mattered — a debate that resurfaced when Pavel Skoropadskyi's circle reclaimed territory.

The 1920s brought European fascism and radical nationalism; thinkers Dmytro Dontsov and Yevhen Malaniuk advanced an elitist Ukrainian identity carried forward by Stepan Bandera and Lev Rebet's generation.

chapters.title: Ukrainian and Russian cultures diverge in mentality and political habit
chapters.text:
Soviet culture was manufactured, yet it still shapes minds — especially among those who grew up in the twentieth century and among foreigners who never looked closer. The USSR and Ukraine are not equivalents: Russia retained imperial continuity under a new name, while Ukraine is a separate republic. Imperial Russians often treat Belarus and Ukraine as temporarily lost provinces.

Surnames signal difference: Russian -ov/-ev endings versus Ukrainian -ko/-uk. Stereotypes flow both ways — Russians may call Ukrainians cunning; Ukrainians may portray Russians as careless drinkers and themselves as industrious craftsmen. Wedding customs differ sharply: Russian feasts sometimes end in brawls documented endlessly online; Ukrainian celebrations more often close with choral song.

Lenin and Stalin's homo Sovieticus project aimed to flatten ethnic diversity — a project that met fierce resistance in Ukraine.

Ukraine has become a nation of independent individualists under Soviet rule.

Political culture diverges too. Russia's monarchic tradition bred tsar cults that persisted in Soviet leadership — kill one ruler, venerate the next. Ukraine skipped monarchy, jumping from princely feudalism to hetman-Cossack governance, with democratic anarchy emerging around the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

Modern Ukrainians vote, campaign, and revolt when leaders betray national interest — a habit stretching back to elected hetmans, while Russians historically accepted rulers as divinely appointed.

Russia's 2014 assault on Donbas and Luhansk invoked protection of Russian speakers — a Russification tactic dating to empire and continuing through Soviet resettlement of officials from Siberia and the Far East. After 1991 independence, national identity needed no restoration; it lived in genes, folklore, and daily tradition.

chapters.title: Crimean Tatars are the peninsula's indigenous people
chapters.text:
Stalin deported roughly 190,000 Crimean Tatars — about half perished during transit or in the first years afterward. After Ukraine's 1991 independence, returnees faced discrimination and narratives casting them as dangerous occupiers of others' land.

Soviet textbooks erased indigenous Crimean history because Tatars refused to abandon religion and custom. The Khan's Palace in Bakhchisaray was reframed as a Russian architectural treasure. Propaganda taught Soviet citizens to forget Tatar existence or treat them as enemies. Property purchases carried inflated prices or outright refusal in the early 1990s. Families nonetheless rebuilt — hosting relatives arriving from other republics, constructing temporary shelters, then permanent homes. Tatars never felt Soviet; the state answered with repression and exile.

Before Catherine II's eighteenth-century annexation, Tatars comprised ninety-five percent of Crimea's population; by 2013, only fourteen percent remained.

Language remains contested. Elders pushed youth to preserve Crimean Tatar despite the ease of Russian media. Post-occupation, only seven of fifteen Tatar schools survive as Moscow promotes a manufactured Crimean people concept — echoing the Soviet homogenization playbook.

Islam anchors community life: Kurban Bayram, Oraza Bayram, wedding customs, and funeral rites persist with very low atheism rates. The nation navigates both Turkic East and European West.

Regardless of their origin, Crimean Tatars consider themselves part of the Ukrainian nation because they share and respect the same values.

Today, the Crimean Tatars play a leading role in the development of the Ukrainian political nation, and are a bright and visible part of the current multicultural and multi-confessional identity of Ukraine. ~ Volodymyr Yermolenko

Volodymyr Yermolenko

Did you know? The Crimean Tatars have three sub-ethnic groups — Yali Boylu (southern coast), Nogai (steppe), and Tata (mountaineers from Bakhchisaray).

chapters.title: Volodymyr Rafayenko's Donbas memories
chapters.text:
Russia's 2014 Donbas war shattered Ukraine's territorial integrity and displaced thousands. Writer Volodymyr Rafayenko — who published in Russian from 1992 through 2018 and won Russian literary prizes — fled Donetsk after militants arrived to protect Russian speakers. He had spoken and studied Russian comfortably for forty-five years in Ukraine, so the premise of the invasion baffled him. Escalating danger forced him to rebuild life elsewhere.

The invasion's shamelessness pushed him to learn Ukrainian and write in it. Rural Donbas spoke Ukrainian; cities favored Russian. Rafayenko labels the regional variant Eastern Ukrainian — distinct from standard Russian. Soviet schools and factories deliberately displaced Ukrainian, so his parents spoke Russian while grandparents retained Ukrainian and memories of the Holodomor famine his youth could not yet grasp.

Russia claims traditional kokoshniks and sundresses for Luhansk and Donetsk, yet local dress featured caps, flower crowns, scarves, and ornamented vyshyvankas.

The war demolished the fraternal-nations myth and sharpened civic identity. Violent ideological conversion backfired. Rafayenko forever associates the Donetsk-Kyiv night train with the line between before and after.

Soviet authorities isolated Donbas to control industry; Russia later exploited that separation in propaganda. The divide was artificial: Ukrainian fairy tales, songs, and Christmas customs remained central to daily life. Residents never considered themselves Russian Federation citizens, even as seventy Soviet years layered Russian urban culture over a Ukrainian core.

chapters.title: Communication and resilience underpin Ukraine's security
chapters.text:
Ukraine spans more than a thousand kilometers east to west — with Europe's geographic center on its western edge — and counts roughly forty-six million citizens, making it Europe's second-largest country by area.

From the 1990s through the 2000s, outsiders treated Ukraine as a buffer zone neither fully Eastern nor Western — a narrative Russia cultivated because ambiguity served its interests. The West hesitated to invest in an environment deemed unstable. Today, in-betweenness replaces buffer-zone language, denying Ukrainian agency and recasting the state as an object of neighbors' foreign policy. Such myths spread fast and linger in public discourse.

Another Kremlin line insists you cannot fight Russia. The opening days of the 2022 full-scale invasion exposed that as bluster. Freedom and future security justify the cost — even when international bodies, distant from the front, still echo intimidation to preserve relationships.

Frozen conflict may be the worst outcome: it postpones resolution while populations acclimate to permanent war. A catch-22 emerges — citizens ready for peace, governments deadlocked; later, leaders fear voters will reject any compromise. Federalizing annexed territories repeats centuries of partition Ukraine cannot accept.

Building resilience and international partnerships beats resigning a nation to buffer-state status.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Ukraine's past and present remain opaque to much of the world. Russia's February 24, 2022 invasion dominated headlines for months, yet sustained attention to Ukrainian history and culture matters beyond the news cycle. Despite war, the country advances — one of Europe's largest states, rooted in ninth-century Kyivan Rus and endowed with black soil that makes it a major exporter of grain, sugar, vegetables, fruits, and oil.

Those lands drew settlers who battled nomads; conquerors who stayed adopted predecessors' customs. Norse founders of Rus accepted Christianity and baptized pagan subjects.

Cossacks anchor Ukrainian statehood; the very word Ukraine denotes borderland governance. Shevchenko and others enshrined the Cossack myth in literature.

Soviet propaganda insisted Ukrainians and Russians were one people — a lie exposed by history and lived culture. The homo Sovieticus project erased ethnic distinction, nowhere more brutally than in Stalin's deportation of Crimean Tatars, who still face oppression under Russian occupation.

Moscow's 2014 Donbas incursion and the 2022 invasion united Ukrainians against their eastern neighbor. Resilience, global communication, and values worth defending sustain daily resistance.

Try this



• Check out the Making of Modern Ukraine course from Yale University to learn even more about Ukraine's history. The English course is available on YouTube.

• Get acquainted with the work of modern Ukrainian artists.
